report_metadata.disclaimer: This document is generated for research and educational purposes only. It is NOT medical advice. All treatment decisions must be made in consultation with a qualified oncology team who can evaluate the patient's individual circumstances. Never start, stop, or change treatment based solely on this document.

treatments.name: Radical Surgical Resection with Lingual Nerve Tracing and Negative Margin Clearance

evidence_level.rationale: Surgery is the cornerstone of ACC management per NCCN v1.2026 and ASCO salivary gland guidelines. Level I evidence from multiple large retrospective series (>1000 patients cumulative) confirming radical surgery with margin-negative resection as primary treatment.

survival_benefit.rationale: Complete resection with negative margins associated with 5-year OS of 82-90%. For T3 disease, R0 resection yields 5-year local-regional control of 70-80%. However, the perineural invasion along the lingual nerve mandates nerve tracing at surgery back toward foramen ovale to ensure complete extirpation.

accessibility.rationale: Available at all major head and neck surgical centers. Requires experienced head and neck surgeon capable of skull base approaches for nerve tracing along V3 pathway.

safety_profile.rationale: Expected morbidity includes lingual nerve sacrifice with tongue numbness/taste loss on affected side, possible marginal mandibular nerve injury. Hypoglossal nerve (XII) at risk given submandibular location. Functional rehabilitation feasible with speech therapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability regardless of molecular profile. MYB-NFIB fusion status does not alter surgical approach. All T3N0M0 ACC patients are surgical candidates.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision of the primary tumor with en-bloc resection of the submandibular gland, floor of mouth contents, and tracing of the lingual nerve (V3 branch) proximally toward the skull base to achieve negative neural margins. In ACC with perineural invasion along a named nerve, intraoperative frozen section of the proximal nerve stump is critical to confirm clearance.

key_evidence.study_name: ASCO Guideline: Management of Salivary Gland Malignancy

notable_side_effects: Lingual nerve sacrifice with ipsilateral tongue numbness and taste loss
Possible marginal mandibular nerve injury (lip asymmetry)
Floor of mouth wound healing complications

treatments.name: Adjuvant Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT) with Perineural Pathway Coverage to Skull Base

evidence_level.rationale: Strong retrospective evidence from multiple large institutional series. NCCN Category 2A recommendation for adjuvant RT in ACC with perineural invasion. Multiple studies demonstrate improved local control with postoperative RT (5-year OS 82.4% vs 72.5% surgery alone).

survival_benefit.rationale: Postoperative RT to 60 Gy (66 Gy for positive margins) significantly improves local-regional control. For PNI along lingual nerve, radiation field must trace the V3 pathway through foramen ovale to the skull base. 5-year local-regional control 75-88% with adjuvant RT.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at radiation oncology centers. Requires expertise in head and neck contouring for perineural pathway delineation. Elective neural coverage of V3 from tumor bed to foramen ovale is critical but standard practice at experienced centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Treatment to 60-66 Gy with perineural pathway coverage entails risk of xerostomia, mucositis, osteoradionecrosis of mandible, and late cranial neuropathies when treating toward skull base. Long follow-up needed given ACC's 20+ year recurrence risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally indicated for all ACC with perineural invasion regardless of molecular profile. Particularly critical in this case given named nerve (lingual nerve) involvement.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon radiation delivered via intensity-modulated technique to the surgical bed and along the perineural pathway of the lingual nerve (V3) extending to the skull base (foramen ovale). The target volume encompasses the submandibular bed, ipsilateral floor of mouth, mandibular nerve pathway through the infratemporal fossa, and foramen ovale. Dose: 60 Gy to tumor bed, 50-54 Gy elective coverage of neural pathway, with boost to 66 Gy for positive or close margins.

key_evidence.study_name: Radiation Therapy for Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma of the Head and Neck

notable_side_effects: Late cranial neuropathies with skull base coverage

treatments.name: Adjuvant Proton Beam Therapy with Perineural Pathway Coverage

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective registry data from 56 patients showing excellent outcomes. 5-year OS 89%, local-regional control 88%. No randomized comparison to photon IMRT, but dosimetric advantages well-established for skull base coverage scenarios.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 89%, cause-specific survival 89%, local-regional control 88%, and disease-free survival 78% in a prospective registry. Particularly advantageous for PNI cases requiring skull base coverage where protons can spare brainstem, temporal lobes, and cochlea while delivering therapeutic dose to foramen ovale.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers are limited, with approximately 45 centers in the US. Insurance coverage may require prior authorization and documentation of dosimetric advantage over photons. Geographic barriers significant for some patients.

safety_profile.rationale: 5-year cumulative incidence of grade 3+ late toxicity was 15% in the prospective registry. Superior normal tissue sparing vs IMRT for skull base targets. Reduced risk of temporal lobe necrosis and cochlear damage compared to photon RT when treating V3 pathway to foramen ovale.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable regardless of molecular profile. Particularly beneficial in this specific case where the lingual nerve PNI requires radiation coverage to the skull base — the proton Bragg peak provides optimal dose conformality around foramen ovale.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beam radiation exploits the Bragg peak to deliver high-dose radiation to the surgical bed and perineural pathway (lingual nerve to foramen ovale/skull base) while minimizing dose to surrounding critical structures including brainstem, temporal lobes, cochlea, and contralateral salivary glands. Median dose 72.6 GyRBE. Dosimetrically superior to photon IMRT when treating near the skull base, reducing late toxicity risk given the 20+ year surveillance needed for ACC.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-Term Outcomes Following Definitive or Adjuvant Proton Radiotherapy for Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics

notable_side_effects: Optic neuropathy (1.8% when optic apparatus in field)
Xerostomia — typically less than photon IMRT
treatments.availability: Available at ~45 US proton centers; referral and insurance authorization required

treatments.name: Lenvatinib (Lenvima) for Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial (n=32) demonstrated ORR 15.6%, median PFS 17.5 months. NCCN Category 2B recommendation for progressive R/M ACC. Real-world Italian data (n=46) confirmed ORR 21.7%. No phase III RCT exists.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 17.5 months in phase II trial, with 75% achieving stable disease. Median OS approximately 27 months. 66% of patients experienced tumor regression. Represents best systemic therapy data available for R/M ACC. Reserved for this patient only if recurrence/metastasis develops.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for other indications (thyroid cancer, HCC, RCC, endometrial cancer); off-label use for ACC supported by NCCN 2B recommendation. Available commercially. Insurance coverage variable for off-label use.

safety_profile.rationale: 62.5% experienced grade 3-4 toxicity. Hypertension grade 3 in 28.1%. 71.9% required dose reduction. Significant toxicity burden including oral pain, fatigue, diarrhea, proteinuria. One grade 4 myocardial infarction and one posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome reported.

biomarker_match.rationale: No specific biomarker required. Interestingly, patients with lower MYB expression (<=10% staining) showed better responses (p=0.026). This patient's MYB-NFIB fusion status does not predict differential response but confirms the ACC diagnosis.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor blocking VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRα, RET, and KIT. Inhibits tumor angiogenesis and directly targets tumor cell proliferative signaling. The anti-angiogenic mechanism is particularly relevant in ACC which demonstrates high vascularity. Dose: 24 mg orally daily in 28-day cycles.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib in Patients With Progressive, Recurrent or Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: None required; NCCN 2B for progressive R/M ACC
treatments.availability: FDA-approved (off-label for ACC); NCCN Category 2B recommendation

treatments.name: REM-422 (MYB mRNA Degrader) — First-in-Class MYB-Targeted Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 trial (NCT06118086) with positive preliminary data presented at AACR-NCI-EORTC 2025. ORR 43% in efficacy population is unprecedented for ACC. FDA Fast Track designation granted March 2026. Still dose-escalation/expansion phase.

survival_benefit.rationale: 43% ORR is the highest response rate ever reported for systemic therapy in ACC. In biomarker-positive patients on study >6 months, 71% (10/14) demonstrated >20% tumor shrinkage with 6 partial responses. Survival data still maturing. Transformative potential if confirmed.

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial only. Available at select sites including MSKCC, Dana-Farber, UCSF. FDA Fast Track designation may accelerate approval. Orphan Drug status granted. Not yet available outside trial enrollment.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated at recommended Phase 2 dose. Most common AEs: anemia, fatigue, epistaxis. No dose-limiting toxicities highlighted in preliminary data. Favorable safety profile compared to multi-TKI approaches.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets MYB — the key oncogenic driver in ACC. This patient's MYB-NFIB fusion makes them an ideal candidate. REM-422 induces poison exon incorporation in MYB mRNA leading to nonsense-mediated decay. Biomarker selection (MYB expression via poison exon) enriches responders.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: First-in-class oral small molecule mRNA degrader that induces reduction of MYB mRNA and protein expression. REM-422 facilitates incorporation of a poison exon in the MYB mRNA transcript, leading to nonsense-mediated decay of the transcript. This directly targets the MYB-NFIB fusion oncoprotein that drives ACC pathogenesis. Represents the first therapy to directly drug the MYB oncogene.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 1 Study of REM-422 in Patients with Recurrent or Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma (NCT06118086)

biomarker_requirements: MYB transcript expression with poison exon (enrichment biomarker)
treatments.availability: Phase 1 Clinical Trial — FDA Fast Track and Orphan Drug designated

treatments.name: Axitinib (Inlyta) for Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial data showing ORR 9.1%, median PFS 4.8 months. Inferior to lenvatinib in head-to-head comparison at same institution (ORR 9.1% vs 15.6%; PFS 4.8 vs 17.1 months). Limited evidence base.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 4.8 months, inferior to lenvatinib. ORR only 9.1%. Disease stabilization observed but responses not durable. Considered second-line after lenvatinib failure or for patients unable to tolerate lenvatinib.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for RCC; off-label for ACC. Commercially available. NCCN does not specifically list axitinib but acknowledges anti-angiogenic TKIs as a class.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than lenvatinib in some patients. Standard VEGFR-TKI toxicity profile: hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome. Generally manageable with dose modifications.

biomarker_match.rationale: No specific biomarker required. Mechanism (VEGFR inhibition) is relevant to ACC vascularity. Alternative option for patients intolerant of lenvatinib.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR-1, VEGFR-2, and VEGFR-3 at sub-nanomolar doses. More selective than lenvatinib with fewer off-target kinase effects. Anti-angiogenic mechanism disrupts tumor vasculature in ACC. Dose: 5 mg BID with escalation to 10 mg BID if tolerated.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Axitinib in Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for RCC; off-label use for ACC

treatments.name: Concurrent Cisplatin Chemoradiation (RTOG 1008 Paradigm)

evidence_level.rationale: RTOG 1008 phase II/III trial ongoing, investigating cisplatin added to postoperative RT for high-risk salivary gland cancers. Retrospective data show 80% complete remission rate with concurrent cisplatin-RT for locally advanced ACC. Role remains controversial per 2025 propensity-matched analysis showing no survival benefit of adding chemotherapy to postoperative IMRT.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 87%, PFS 39%, LPFS 61% in unresectable ACC treated with CCRT. For postoperative high-risk ACC, propensity-matched data suggest no clear benefit over RT alone. Potential benefit limited to highest-risk subset (positive margins, solid histology).

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and IMRT both widely available. Can be administered at any academic medical center or community oncology practice with radiation capabilities.

safety_profile.rationale: Added cisplatin toxicity on top of radiation: enhanced mucositis, nephrotoxicity requiring hydration, ototoxicity, nausea. Grade 3 oral mucositis in 10% when cisplatin added. Increased acute morbidity versus RT alone.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-driven. May be considered for this patient given high-risk features (T3, PNI along named nerve) if RTOG 1008 enrollment available, or if positive margins after surgery.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Concurrent administration of cisplatin (radiosensitizer) during postoperative IMRT. Cisplatin creates DNA crosslinks that impair radiation damage repair, enhancing tumor cell kill. Standard dosing: cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks x 3 cycles during radiation or weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2. Evaluated in RTOG 1008 for high-risk salivary gland cancers.

key_evidence.study_name: Role of concurrent chemoradiation on locally advanced unresectable adenoid cystic carcinoma

treatments.availability: Available as off-protocol regimen; RTOG 1008 trial may be enrolling

treatments.name: Cisplatin-Doxorubicin-Cyclophosphamide (CAP) Combination Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple phase II studies establish CAP as the most studied chemotherapy regimen for R/M ACC. ORR 18-33% across studies. No phase III data. Responses generally short-lived.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 5.7 months, median OS 15.1-23.4 months in different series. Responses not durable with median time to progression after CAP of 6.6 months. Disease stabilization in majority but true objective responses uncommon.

accessibility.rationale: All three agents are generic, widely available, and FDA-approved for other indications. Any oncology center can administer this regimen. Insurance coverage straightforward.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity from cisplatin, cardiotoxicity from doxorubicin (cumulative dose limit 450 mg/m2), nausea/vomiting, alopecia, and infection risk. Platinum-based toxicity considerable especially in prolonged treatment.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-selective cytotoxic chemotherapy applicable to all ACC subtypes regardless of molecular profile. No biomarker enrichment available.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple cytotoxic combination: Cisplatin (DNA crosslinking), Doxorubicin (topoisomerase II inhibition and DNA intercalation), and Cyclophosphamide (alkylating agent). Non-targeted approach causing broad DNA damage and cell death. Reserved for progressive, symptomatic recurrent/metastatic ACC when anti-angiogenic TKIs have failed or rapid tumor response is needed.

key_evidence.study_name: Efficacy of cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and cisplatin for adenoid cystic carcinoma

treatments.availability: Widely available — all agents generic and FDA-approved

treatments.name: All-Trans Retinoic Acid (ATRA) — MYB Expression Modulator

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial showed 0% ORR but 61% stable disease rate with median stability of 3.7 months. ATRA inhibits MYB expression and suppresses MYB-NFIB fusion protein. Preclinical evidence of suppression of lung metastasis via NOTCH1 pathway modulation.

survival_benefit.rationale: No objective responses in phase II monotherapy trial. Disease stabilization in 61% but median duration only 3.7 months. Combination with apatinib showed 100% 6-month SD rate and 80% 12-month stability. Benefit is primarily disease stabilization, not shrinkage.

accessibility.rationale: ATRA is FDA-approved for acute promyelocytic leukemia and widely available as generic tretinoin capsules. Oral administration. Off-label for ACC but inexpensive and accessible.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated with mild side effects: dry skin, cheilitis, headache, hypertriglyceridemia. Low-grade toxicity profile makes it attractive for long-term maintenance or combination strategies.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets MYB expression pathway. This patient's MYB-NFIB fusion makes ATRA mechanistically relevant. Low MYB protein expression correlated with longer duration of stability on ATRA (potential response biomarker).

treatments.mechanism_of_action: All-trans retinoic acid inhibits MYB expression and dramatically lowers levels of the MYB-NFIB oncogenic fusion protein. ATRA also suppresses lung metastasis of ACC by correcting erroneous cell differentiation caused by aberrant NOTCH1 or MYB expression. Promotes terminal differentiation of ACC cells. Oral capsule formulation at 45 mg/m2/day.

key_evidence.study_name: A phase II trial of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) in advanced adenoid cystic carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: MYB-NFIB fusion or MYB overexpression (enrichment biomarker)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for APL; off-label for ACC, widely available generic

treatments.name: SBRT for Oligometastatic Disease (SOLAR Trial Paradigm)

evidence_level.rationale: SOLAR phase II trial (NCT04883671) investigating early SBRT for oligometastatic ACC (1-3 sites). Limited published outcomes data. Concept supported by retrospective series showing local control of lung metastases with SBRT. Proton SBRT case report showed high-dose tumor control with organ preservation in 16 pulmonary metastases.

survival_benefit.rationale: Hypothesis: early ablation of oligometastatic sites may delay systemic therapy need and extend PFS. ACC's indolent course makes oligometastatic management particularly relevant — many patients live years with limited lung metastases. Direct survival data from SOLAR trial not yet mature.

accessibility.rationale: SBRT widely available at radiation oncology centers. SOLAR trial enrolling at UCSF and partner sites. Off-protocol SBRT for ACC lung metastases increasingly offered at academic centers.

safety_profile.rationale: SBRT for lung metastases is well-tolerated with low toxicity rates. Minimal impact on pulmonary function for limited number of treated lesions. Non-invasive outpatient treatment in 3-5 fractions.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Applicable to all ACC patients developing oligometastatic disease. Particularly relevant given ACC's 50% distant metastasis rate over long follow-up.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Stereotactic body radiotherapy delivers ablative doses of radiation (typically 48-60 Gy in 3-5 fractions) to individual metastatic deposits, achieving local tumor destruction with minimal normal tissue exposure. For ACC's characteristically indolent pulmonary metastases, SBRT may control individual lesions for years, delaying need for systemic therapy. The SOLAR trial specifically tests whether early SBRT to oligometastatic sites impacts ACC's typically prolonged metastatic course.

key_evidence.study_name: SOLAR: Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy for Early Treatment of Oligometastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma (NCT04883671)

treatments.availability: Phase II trial (SOLAR, NCT04883671) at UCSF; off-protocol SBRT widely available

treatments.name: Carbon Ion Radiotherapy (CIRT) for Recurrent or Unresectable Disease

evidence_level.rationale: Systematic review of 12 CIRT studies for ACC. 5-year local control 73-90% in specialized centers. Higher RBE (~3.0) than photons or protons. No randomized data vs proton or photon RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 63%, PFS 39%, local control 75% in combined proton/carbon ion analysis. CIRT may be particularly effective for radioresistant solid-pattern ACC due to enhanced biological effectiveness. Primarily relevant for recurrent disease or unresectable primary tumors.

accessibility.rationale: No carbon ion facilities currently operational in the US. Available in Japan (NIRS/QST), Germany (HIT Heidelberg), Italy (CNAO), and China. Requires international travel. Insurance coverage unlikely for US patients.

safety_profile.rationale: Moderate toxicities remain within acceptable range in published series. Higher RBE means enhanced normal tissue effects near target. Late toxicities need long-term follow-up given ACC's protracted natural history.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Particularly relevant for ACC given its relative radioresistance with conventional photon therapy. Carbon ion's high-LET radiation may overcome radioresistance mechanisms.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Carbon ions combine the dosimetric advantages of charged particle beams (Bragg peak) with enhanced radiobiological effectiveness (RBE ~3.0 vs 1.1 for protons). High linear energy transfer (LET) creates clustered DNA damage that is less reparable than photon-induced damage, potentially overcoming the relative radioresistance of ACC. Particularly suited for skull base extension and re-irradiation scenarios.

key_evidence.study_name: A systematic review of particle-based treatment modalities for adenoid cystic carcinomas of the head and neck

notable_side_effects: Soft tissue necrosis at high doses
treatments.availability: International centers only (Japan, Germany, Italy, China); not available in US

treatments.name: Nivolumab plus Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial NCT03146650 (n=19 ACC patients) showed modest activity. ORR 5% (1 PR), DCR 42.1%. Median OS 30.0 months, median PFS 8.3 months. Trial prematurely terminated due to funding. ACC is generally immunologically cold with low TMB and PD-L1.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 30.0 months and PFS 8.3 months compare favorably to historical controls. One durable PR lasting >48 months. However, ORR of only 5% is very low. The long OS may reflect ACC's indolent natural history rather than treatment effect.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved for other indications (melanoma, RCC, NSCLC). Off-label use for ACC not well-supported. Limited insurance coverage for off-label dual checkpoint immunotherapy in ACC.

safety_profile.rationale: 45.8% experienced grade III-IV adverse events. Immune-related AEs include adrenal insufficiency, colitis, hepatitis. Treatment discontinuation in 12.5% due to AEs. Dual checkpoint blockade carries higher toxicity than single agent.

biomarker_match.rationale: ACC is typically PD-L1 negative, TMB-low, and immune-desert. This patient's NOTCH1 WT status and MYB-NFIB fusion are not predictive of immunotherapy response. The one PR in the trial had BCOR frameshift mutations, suggesting niche applicability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual immune checkpoint blockade: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) restores T-cell activity against tumor, while Ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) enhances T-cell priming and activation. Combined blockade may overcome the 'immune-desert' microenvironment of ACC. However, ACC's characteristically low TMB and absence of PD-L1 expression limit efficacy in most patients.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II study of nivolumab and ipilimumab for treatment of metastatic/recurrent adenoid cystic carcinoma (NCT03146650)

biomarker_requirements: No established predictive biomarker; BCOR mutations may predict response (hypothesis-generating)
treatments.availability: Off-label; both agents FDA-approved for other indications

treatments.name: Dovitinib (FGFR/VEGFR Inhibitor) for Recurrent/Metastatic ACC

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial with PR in 2 patients, 65% disease stabilization, median PFS 8.5 months. Targets FGFR1 pathway which may be relevant in MYB-NFIB fusion ACC. Limited sample size.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 8.5 months with 65% disease stabilization. Responses modest but PFS compares favorably to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Potential niche for FGFR-altered ACC.

accessibility.rationale: Not FDA-approved. Clinical trial access only or compassionate use. Development has been limited for ACC indication specifically.

safety_profile.rationale: Multi-TKI toxicity profile: nausea, diarrhea, fatigue, hypertension, elevated liver enzymes. Manageable but requires monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: FGFR pathway involvement in ACC pathogenesis is established. MYB-NFIB fusion may activate downstream FGFR signaling. No validated companion diagnostic.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against FGFR1-3, VEGFR1-3, PDGFRβ, and FLT3. Targets fibroblast growth factor receptor signaling pathway which plays a role in ACC cell proliferation and survival, particularly in tumors with FGFR amplification or MYB-driven FGFR upregulation.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II study of Dovitinib in Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma

biomarker_requirements: No required biomarker; FGFR pathway activation may enrich for response
treatments.availability: Clinical trial or compassionate use only

treatments.name: AL101 (Osugacestat) Gamma-Secretase Inhibitor (NOTCH-Mutant ACC Only)
treatments.category: Investigational — Not Applicable to This Patient

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 ACCURACY trial (n=82) showed ORR 12%, DCR 69% in NOTCH-mutant R/M ACC. Final results presented at ESMO 2023. Meaningful clinical activity in biomarker-selected population.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 12% with 57% stable disease. Moderate disease control but limited objective responses. Data relevant only to NOTCH-mutant ACC (~20% of cases).

accessibility.rationale: Available only through clinical trials. Requires NOTCH activating mutation — this patient is NOTCH1 wild-type, making them INELIGIBLE for AL101.

safety_profile.rationale: Diarrhea 78% (grade 3: 10%), fatigue 67% (grade 3: 6%), nausea 60% (grade 3: 7%). GI toxicity is substantial due to NOTCH pathway inhibition in gut epithelium.

biomarker_match.rationale: CRITICAL: This patient is NOTCH1 wild-type. AL101 requires NOTCH1-4 activating mutations for efficacy. This therapy is NOT indicated for this patient. Included for completeness as a key ACC investigational agent, but biomarker mismatch precludes use.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pan-NOTCH gamma-secretase inhibitor that prevents cleavage and nuclear translocation of activated NOTCH receptors. NOTCH pathway activation (via mutations in NOTCH1-4) occurs in ~20% of ACC and is associated with aggressive, solid-pattern disease. AL101 blocks all four NOTCH receptors. CRITICAL NOTE: This patient is NOTCH1 WT and would not benefit from this therapy — NOTCH activating mutations are required for response.

key_evidence.study_name: ACCURACY: Phase 2 trial of AL101 in R/M ACC with NOTCH activating mutations

biomarker_requirements: NOTCH1, NOTCH2, NOTCH3, or NOTCH4 activating mutation REQUIRED
This patient is NOTCH1 WT — NOT ELIGIBLE
treatments.availability: Clinical trial only; requires NOTCH mutation — patient INELIGIBLE (NOTCH1 WT)

treatments.name: Vorinostat (HDAC Inhibitor) with Cisplatin Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Vorinostat monotherapy showed only 3% ORR in clinical trial. However, preclinical data demonstrate vorinostat + cisplatin combination is extremely effective at depleting cancer stem cells and inducing senescence in ACC primary cells. Clinical combination data lacking.

survival_benefit.rationale: Monotherapy showed minimal clinical benefit (3% ORR). Combination with cisplatin has strong preclinical rationale (cancer stem cell depletion, tumor senescence) but no clinical survival data. Purely hypothesis-generating.

accessibility.rationale: Vorinostat is FDA-approved for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. Off-label use possible. Cisplatin widely available. Combination use would be off-protocol.

safety_profile.rationale: Vorinostat causes thrombocytopenia, fatigue, nausea, and diarrhea. Combined with cisplatin, overlapping toxicities (myelosuppression, GI) would be additive. No clinical safety data for the combination in ACC.

biomarker_match.rationale: HDAC inhibition unlocks chromatin in ACC tumors, sensitizing cancer stem cells to cisplatin. Epigenetic mechanism may be synergistic with MYB-driven transcriptional programs. No validated biomarker.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorinostat (suberoylanilide hydroxamic acid) inhibits histone deacetylases classes I and II, unlocking chromatin structure in ACC cells. This epigenetic remodeling sensitizes cancer stem cells to cisplatin-induced DNA damage and activates cellular senescence pathways. The combination targets the cancer stem cell population thought to drive ACC's characteristic late recurrences over 20+ years.

key_evidence.study_name: Unlocking the chromatin of adenoid cystic carcinomas using HDAC inhibitors sensitize cancer stem cells to cisplatin and induces tumor senescence

treatments.availability: Both agents FDA-approved for other indications; combination use off-label/off-protocol

clinical_trials.title: Phase 1 Study of REM-422, a MYB mRNA Degrader, in Patients with Recurrent or Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma
sites: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (New York, NY)
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 43% in efficacy population. 71% of biomarker-positive patients on study >6 months showed >20% tumor shrinkage. FDA Fast Track and Orphan Drug designated. First-in-class MYB-targeted therapy — highly relevant for this MYB-NFIB fusion+ patient.

clinical_trials.title: SOLAR: Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy (SBRT) for Early Treatment of Oligometastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Investigating whether early SBRT for 1-3 oligometastatic sites impacts PFS, OS, and quality of life in advanced ACC. Relevant if this patient develops distant metastases during 20+ year surveillance.

clinical_trials.title: Randomized Phase II/III Study of Adjuvant Concurrent Radiation and Cisplatin vs. Radiation Alone in Resected High-Risk Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors
clinical_trials.status: Active, enrollment status varies by site
sites: Multiple NRG Oncology sites across the US
biomarker_requirements: High-risk salivary gland cancer (including high-grade ACC with >30% solid component)
clinical_trials.early_results: Results pending. If positive, may establish cisplatin + RT as new standard for high-risk salivary gland cancers. This patient's T3 PNI+ disease may qualify depending on histologic grading.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Nivolumab and Ipilimumab for Treatment of Metastatic/Recurrent Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: 19 ACC patients: ORR 5%, DCR 42.1%, median OS 30.0 months, median PFS 8.3 months. One durable PR >48 months in patient with BCOR mutations. Modest efficacy in an immunologically cold tumor.

clinical_trials.title: Study of Amivantamab in Patients with Recurrent and Metastatic Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Novel bispecific antibody targeting EGFR and MET. EGFR overexpression found in >85% of ACC. Early clinical data pending. Represents new approach given failure of single-target EGFR inhibitors.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Trial of AL101 (Osugacestat) Gamma-Secretase Inhibitor in R/M ACC with NOTCH Activating Mutations
biomarker_requirements: Patient is NOTCH1 WT — NOT ELIGIBLE
clinical_trials.early_results: 82 patients enrolled. ORR 12% (9 PR), DCR 69%. Diarrhea (78%), fatigue (67%), nausea (60%). NOT applicable to this NOTCH1 WT patient but important reference for NOTCH-mutant ACC.

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Adjuvant IMRT/Proton Therapy with Perineural Pathway Coverage (60-66 Gy)
combination_strategies.rationale: STANDARD APPROACH for this patient. Surgery resects gross disease with lingual nerve tracing to negative margins, then adjuvant RT covers the surgical bed and traces the V3 perineural pathway to the skull base (foramen ovale). Covers microscopic residual disease along neural sheaths. 5-year local-regional control improves from ~60% (surgery alone) to 75-88% (surgery + RT). PNI along a named nerve is an absolute indication for adjuvant RT per NCCN.

Adding cisplatin radiosensitization to postoperative RT may improve local control in highest-risk patients (T3, PNI+, positive margins). 80% complete remission in small unresectable series. However, recent propensity-matched data show no survival benefit in postoperative setting. Consider if surgical margins are positive or close after nerve tracing.

Combination of anti-angiogenic TKI with checkpoint immunotherapy was hypothesized to convert immunologically cold ACC to responsive. Phase II trial failed to show ORR advantage over lenvatinib alone. The anti-angiogenic effects may paradoxically impair immune cell infiltration. NOT recommended based on available evidence.

ATRA suppresses MYB expression while apatinib inhibits angiogenesis. Combination showed 100% 6-month stable disease rate and 80% 12-month stability rate, superior to either agent alone. Mechanistic synergy: ATRA-mediated differentiation combined with anti-angiogenic vascular normalization. MYB-NFIB fusion+ patients like this one may preferentially benefit.

HDAC inhibition unlocks chromatin, exposing DNA to cisplatin damage. Combination depletes ACC cancer stem cells and induces cellular senescence. Targets the stem cell reservoir thought to drive ACC's characteristic 20+ year late recurrences. Clinical translation needed.

supportive_care.approach: Lingual Nerve Rehabilitation and Speech-Swallowing Therapy
supportive_care.evidence: Multidisciplinary head and neck rehabilitation guidelines; institutional series on cranial nerve rehabilitation outcomes
supportive_care.benefit: Lingual nerve sacrifice during surgery will cause ipsilateral tongue numbness and taste loss. Early speech-language pathology intervention improves swallowing function, speech clarity, and dietary tolerance. Neurosensory recovery may occur over 6-18 months via contralateral nerve compensation. Focused tongue exercises and sensory retraining protocols facilitate adaptation.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple retrospective ACC series showing recurrence beyond 10-20 years; Cleveland Clinic ACC guidelines; NCCN salivary gland surveillance recommendations
supportive_care.benefit: ACC has unique biology with late recurrences at 10, 15, and even 20+ years post-treatment. 5-year disease-specific survival of 89% drops to 40% at 15 years. Recommended surveillance: clinical exam and cross-sectional imaging (CT chest, MRI head/neck) every 3-6 months for years 1-2, every 6 months for years 3-5, then annually LIFELONG. Lung metastases (most common distant site, ~50% lifetime risk) may be detected early and managed with SBRT or observation given indolent biology.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Survivorship Guidelines; Cochrane systematic reviews on xerostomia interventions
supportive_care.benefit: Adjuvant RT to submandibular bed and perineural pathway will likely cause significant xerostomia. Management includes: salivary substitutes, pilocarpine (5 mg TID) or cevimeline (30 mg TID) for salivary stimulation, rigorous dental care with fluoride trays (radiation caries prevention), and humidification during sleep. Proton therapy may reduce xerostomia severity vs IMRT by sparing contralateral salivary glands.

supportive_care.approach: Psychosocial Support and ACC-Specific Patient Resources
supportive_care.evidence: Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma Research Foundation (ACCRF) and ACC Organization International (ACCOI) patient support programs
supportive_care.benefit: ACC's rarity (~1,200 US cases/year) and unique challenge of 20+ year recurrence risk create significant psychological burden not typical of other head and neck cancers. ACC-specific patient advocacy organizations provide peer support, clinical trial navigation, and educational resources. Psychological screening for cancer-related distress recommended per NCCN distress management guidelines. Young age (45) amplifies concerns about long-term surveillance burden, work capacity, and family planning.

supportive_care.evidence: ADA guidelines for oral care of patients receiving head and neck radiation; Prosthodontic literature on radiation-related complications
supportive_care.benefit: Radiation to the submandibular region and floor of mouth increases risk of radiation caries, osteoradionecrosis of mandible (5-10% lifetime risk), and trismus. Pre-radiation comprehensive dental evaluation with extraction of compromised teeth required. Post-treatment: custom fluoride trays, 3-monthly dental visits, hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) consideration before any post-RT dental extractions. Trismus prevention with jaw exercises (TheraBite or similar device).

supportive_care.approach: Nutritional Optimization During and After Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines on nutrition in cancer patients; Head and neck cancer nutritional intervention studies
supportive_care.benefit: Surgery and radiation to floor of mouth/submandibular region impact swallowing and taste. Prophylactic PEG tube consideration if significant weight loss anticipated. High-calorie supplementation during RT. Post-treatment dietary counseling for texture-modified diet during recovery. Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) for wound healing. Monitoring for vitamin D and micronutrient deficiencies during long-term follow-up.
